exercises.title: Agno and Mastra as production runtimes
exercises.prompt: Agno (Python) and Mastra (TypeScript) are the 2026 production-runtime pairing. Identify Agno's performance targets (when they matter) and name Mastra's three primitives (Agents, Tools, Workflows) and its substrate.
exercises.hint: Agno: microsecond agent instantiation, stateless FastAPI backends — matters for high-throughput/serverless where you create agents per request. Mastra: Agents, Tools, Workflows on the Vercel AI SDK substrate, with unified model routing and composite storage.
exercises.solution:
**Agno (Python) — performance targets and when they matter:** Agno aims at **microsecond agent instantiation** and **stateless FastAPI backends**:
- **Microsecond agent instantiation:** creating an agent is **extremely fast** — so you can **instantiate agents per request** without overhead. 
- **Stateless, session-scoped FastAPI backends:** agents run in **stateless backends** (state lives in a session store, not in the process), so the backend can **scale horizontally** (any instance handles any request, Phase 13.9's statelessness).
*When these matter:* **high-throughput, serverless, or per-request agent creation** — if you're spinning up an agent for **every incoming request** at scale (a web backend serving many users), microsecond instantiation and stateless scaling are critical (you can't afford slow agent startup or per-process state when serving thousands of requests). They matter **less** for a single long-running agent (where you create one agent and keep it). The recommended Agno production path is a **stateless, session-scoped FastAPI backend** — fast agent creation per request, state externalized to a session store, horizontally scalable.

**Mastra (TypeScript) — three primitives and substrate:** Mastra is the **TypeScript production runtime**:
- **Three primitives:** **Agents** (the agent units), **Tools** (capabilities), **Workflows** (orchestration/control flow — the workflow shape, Lesson 12). The clean agent/tool/workflow decomposition.
- **Substrate:** built on the **Vercel AI SDK** — leveraging that ecosystem's model integrations and TypeScript/web-native tooling. It ships **unified model routing** (one interface across providers, Phase 13.21) and **composite storage** (flexible state/memory backends), plus **server adapters** for deployment.
Mastra is the **TypeScript/web-native** production runtime (Agno's TS counterpart) — Agents + Tools + Workflows on the Vercel AI SDK, for teams building agents in the JS/TS ecosystem.

**The pairing:** **Agno (Python) and Mastra (TypeScript)** are the **2026 production-runtime pairing** — the same role (a production-grade agent runtime with agents/tools/workflows, model routing, storage, and server deployment) in the **two dominant ecosystems** (Python and TypeScript). Pick based on your stack: Agno for Python backends (especially high-throughput/serverless), Mastra for TypeScript/web (Vercel AI SDK ecosystem). Both emphasize **production concerns** — performance, statelessness, model routing, storage, deployment — over research-y orchestration, fitting the 'production runtime' niche.

exercises.title: Stateless session-scoped backends and why they're the production path
exercises.prompt: Explain why a stateless, session-scoped FastAPI backend is the recommended Agno production path. Sketch the pattern (state in a session store, agent created per request, externalized memory) and explain the scaling and reliability benefits versus a stateful long-lived agent process.
exercises.hint: Stateless: the process holds no per-user state; state lives in an external session store. Per request: create the agent (microsecond), load session state, run, persist state. Benefits: horizontal scaling (any instance handles any request), crash resilience (state survives), no memory leaks from long-lived state.
exercises.solution:
**The stateless session-scoped pattern:**
```python
# FastAPI endpoint: stateless, session-scoped
@app.post("/agent/{session_id}")
def handle(session_id: str, request: Request):
    state = session_store.load(session_id)        # load state from EXTERNAL store
    agent = create_agent(state)                    # instantiate per request (microsecond)
    result = agent.run(request.message)
    session_store.save(session_id, agent.state)    # persist state back externally
    return result
# The process holds NO per-user state between requests — state lives in session_store.
```
The pattern: **(1)** each request **loads the session's state from an external store** (keyed by session_id), **(2)** **creates an agent per request** (cheap — microsecond instantiation), **(3)** runs it, **(4)** **persists the updated state back to the external store**. The **backend process itself holds no per-user state between requests** — all durable state lives in the **external session store** (Phase 13.9's externalized state).

**Why stateless session-scoped is the recommended production path:**
- **Horizontal scaling:** because the **process holds no per-user state**, **any backend instance can handle any request** — a user's request #1 might hit instance A, request #2 instance B, and it works because **both load the same session state from the shared store**. This lets you **scale out across a fleet** (add instances behind a load balancer) trivially — the defining benefit of statelessness (Phase 13.9). A **stateful** backend (state in the process) **can't scale this way** — a user must always hit the same instance (sticky sessions), which limits scaling and complicates load balancing.
- **Crash resilience:** if a **backend instance crashes**, **no state is lost** — it's in the external store. The user's next request hits another instance, loads the state, and **continues seamlessly**. A stateful process crashing would **lose all its in-memory state** (the durability problem, Lessons 8, 13). Statelessness + external state = **crash-resilient**.
- **No memory leaks / unbounded growth:** a **long-lived stateful agent process accumulates state** (memory leaks, ever-growing per-user state in RAM) and **degrades over time**. A **stateless per-request backend creates a fresh agent and discards it** each request — **no accumulation, no leaks** (the process stays lean). Microsecond instantiation (Exercise 1) makes per-request creation cheap enough to do this.
- **Simplicity of deployment:** stateless services are **easy to deploy, restart, autoscale, and reason about** (standard cloud-native pattern) — you can kill/restart instances freely.
**Versus a stateful long-lived agent process:** a stateful process (one long-lived agent holding state in memory) is **simpler for a single agent** but **doesn't scale (sticky sessions), loses state on crash, and leaks/grows over time**. The **stateless session-scoped** pattern trades a tiny per-request cost (load/save state, create agent — cheap thanks to microsecond instantiation) for **horizontal scaling, crash resilience, and no state accumulation** — exactly the production properties you need to serve agents at scale. This is why it's Agno's recommended path: **microsecond instantiation makes stateless per-request agents practical, and statelessness is what makes the backend production-grade** (scalable, resilient, leak-free). It's the same lesson as MCP transports (Phase 13.9) and serving infra (Phase 11.13) — **externalize state, keep the compute stateless, scale horizontally.**

exercises.title: Production runtimes vs orchestration frameworks, and choosing a runtime
exercises.prompt: Explain how production runtimes (Agno, Mastra) differ in focus from orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI), how they relate (you can run orchestrated agents on a production runtime), the Python-vs-TypeScript ecosystem choice, and how runtime choice connects to the six production runtime shapes (Lesson 29).
exercises.hint: Runtimes focus on production concerns (perf, statelessness, model routing, storage, deployment, serving); orchestration frameworks focus on agent structure (state graphs, actors, roles). They compose: orchestration logic runs ON a runtime/backend. Python (Agno) vs TS (Mastra) by stack. Runtime shape (Lesson 29) drives the deployment pattern.
exercises.solution:
**Production runtimes vs orchestration frameworks (different focus):**
- **Orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI — Lessons 13-15):** focus on the **agent's *structure and behavior*** — how the agent loop is organized (state graphs, actor message-passing, role-based crews), the orchestration topology, the control flow. They answer **'how is the agent's logic structured?'**
- **Production runtimes (Agno, Mastra — this lesson):** focus on **production *concerns*** — **performance (fast instantiation), statelessness (horizontal scaling), model routing (Phase 13.21), composite storage (state/memory backends), server adapters (deployment), and serving**. They answer **'how do I run agents reliably and at scale in production?'**
The distinction: **orchestration = the agent's internal structure; runtime = the production infrastructure to serve it.** Different layers of the stack.

**How they relate (they compose):** **you can run orchestrated agents on a production runtime** — they're **complementary layers**, not alternatives:
- The **orchestration framework** (LangGraph/AutoGen/CrewAI) defines the **agent's logic** (the state graph, the actors, the crew).
- The **production runtime** (Agno/Mastra) provides the **backend/serving infrastructure** to **deploy and scale** that logic — the stateless FastAPI backend, the model routing, the storage, the deployment adapters.
E.g. you might build the agent's orchestration in LangGraph (durable state graph) and **serve it via a production runtime's stateless backend pattern** (Exercise 2), with the runtime's model routing and storage. The orchestration framework handles *what the agent does and how its logic flows*; the runtime handles *how it's served reliably at scale*. They stack: **orchestration logic runs ON the production runtime/backend.** (Some runtimes also provide light orchestration — workflows — but their emphasis is the production layer.)

**The Python-vs-TypeScript ecosystem choice:** **Agno (Python) and Mastra (TypeScript)** are the **same role in the two dominant ecosystems** — choose by your **stack**:
- **Python (Agno):** if your backend/team is **Python** (the dominant ML/data ecosystem, most agent frameworks are Python). Pairs with FastAPI, the Python AI ecosystem.
- **TypeScript (Mastra):** if you're building **web/full-stack in TypeScript** (the Vercel AI SDK ecosystem, web-native deployment). Pairs with the JS/TS frontend-backend world.
The choice is mostly **'which language/ecosystem is your team and infrastructure in?'** — both provide equivalent production-runtime capabilities; pick the one native to your stack.

**How runtime choice connects to the six production runtime shapes (Lesson 29):** Lesson 29 names **six production runtime shapes** — **request-response, streaming, durable execution, queue-based background, event-driven, scheduled (cron)** — and the key principle: **pick the shape before the framework.** Production runtimes (Agno, Mastra) and orchestration frameworks each **fit different shapes**:
- **Request-response / streaming:** a **stateless session-scoped backend** (Agno's FastAPI pattern, Exercise 2) — fast per-request agents, horizontal scaling. Agno/Mastra excel here.
- **Durable execution (long-horizon, resume-from-failure):** **LangGraph's checkpointed state graph** (Lesson 13) — the durable-execution shape.
- **Queue-based background / event-driven:** queue workers / event handlers (and **Claude Managed Agents** for hosted long-running async, Lesson 17) — the background/event shapes.
- **Scheduled (cron):** scheduled triggers.
The connection: **runtime shape (Lesson 29) determines the deployment pattern, which determines which runtime/framework fits.** A **request-response API** → a stateless production runtime (Agno/Mastra). A **durable long-horizon task** → LangGraph's durable execution. A **background batch** → a queue runtime. **Pick the shape your task needs first** (Lesson 29), then the **production runtime (Agno/Mastra) for the serving/scaling layer** and the **orchestration framework for the agent logic** that fit that shape. The synthesis: production agents need **both an orchestration layer (agent structure — match to problem shape, Phase 11.17) AND a production runtime layer (serving/scaling — match to runtime shape, Lesson 29)**. Agno/Mastra are the **production-runtime layer** (performance, statelessness, routing, storage, deployment) in Python/TypeScript respectively — they make orchestrated agents **reliably servable at scale**, which is the difference between an agent that works in a notebook and one that serves 10,000 users. Choose the runtime by ecosystem (Python→Agno, TS→Mastra) and the deployment pattern by runtime shape (Lesson 29), and compose it with the orchestration framework whose abstraction fits your agent's logic.
